seo.title: Government AI Coding Assistant AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai coding assistant AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying an AI coding assistant within government environments introduces unique risks where a single misconfiguration can expose classified systems, violate civil liberties statutes, or trigger congressional oversight. This checklist addresses the intersection of FedRAMP authorization requirements, FISMA continuous monitoring mandates, and the operational realities of a code-aware LLM agent with sandbox execution capabilities. Every item is calibrated to the critical sensitivity of government IT infrastructure and the legal obligations imposed by the Executive Order on AI and NIST 800-53 controls.

sections.heading: Data Classification & Boundary Controls
sections.description: Ensure the AI coding assistant never processes, transmits, or caches data above its authorized classification level, and that all repository context fed to the LLM is pre-screened for sensitive content.

items.label: Classify all repositories before enabling AI agent access
items.description: Run automated data classification scans on every repository the agent can access, tagging files containing PII, CUI, or any controlled unclassified information before granting the LLM context window access.

items.label: Block agent access to repositories containing classified or SECRET-level code
items.description: Implement a hard technical block—not just a policy control—preventing the code-aware LLM from ingesting any file or commit history tagged at SECRET or above, enforced at the API gateway layer.

items.label: Enforce network segmentation between the LLM inference endpoint and classified networks
items.description: Verify that the sandbox execution environment and LLM API endpoint reside on networks physically or cryptographically isolated from any classified enclave, with no bridging routes permitted.

items.label: Scan all repository context for embedded credentials before LLM ingestion
items.description: Deploy a secrets-detection tool (e.g., truffleHog, detect-secrets) as a pre-processing step that strips or redacts API keys, passwords, certificates, and AWS/Azure credentials from any code chunk sent to the LLM.

items.label: Implement output filtering to prevent credential or sensitive data exfiltration in LLM responses
items.description: Apply regex and ML-based output scanning on all LLM-generated code and text to detect and block any reproduction of secrets, internal IP ranges, or agency-specific configuration values before delivery to the developer.

items.label: Document data flow diagrams showing all paths from government repositories to LLM endpoints
items.description: Produce and maintain an up-to-date data flow diagram (DFD) as required by FedRAMP System Security Plan (SSP) documentation, explicitly mapping every point where code context leaves the agency boundary.

items.label: Establish retention and deletion policies for LLM prompt and completion logs
items.description: Define maximum retention periods for all prompt/completion logs consistent with NARA records schedules and agency SORN, and implement automated deletion with audit confirmation.

sections.heading: FedRAMP & FISMA Authorization Compliance
sections.description: Confirm the AI coding assistant and its underlying LLM infrastructure hold or operate under a valid FedRAMP authorization, and that continuous monitoring obligations under FISMA are actively met.

items.label: Verify the LLM provider holds a FedRAMP Moderate or High authorization
items.description: Check the FedRAMP Marketplace to confirm the specific LLM service and API version in use carries an active FedRAMP authorization at the impact level matching your system's data sensitivity before any production deployment.

items.label: Obtain an Authority to Operate (ATO) or P-ATO amendment covering the AI coding assistant
items.description: Work with your Authorizing Official (AO) to determine whether the AI agent constitutes a significant change to an existing system requiring an ATO amendment, and complete the required security assessment before go-live.

items.label: Implement continuous monitoring for the AI agent per FISMA requirements
items.description: Enroll the AI coding assistant's components—including the sandbox execution environment, LLM API integration, and test validation pipeline—into the agency's continuous monitoring program with monthly vulnerability scans and annual penetration testing.

items.label: Map AI agent controls to NIST 800-53 Rev 5 control families
items.description: Complete a control mapping exercise documenting how the AI coding assistant satisfies or inherits controls across SA (System and Services Acquisition), SI (System and Information Integrity), and AC (Access Control) families.

items.label: Conduct an Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) prior to deployment
items.description: Perform a structured AIA documenting the agent's decision logic, potential for biased code suggestions, impact on developer workflows, and mitigation measures, as required by the Executive Order on AI for federal agency use.

items.label: Establish a Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) for any open AI-specific security findings
items.description: Create POA&M entries for any unresolved risks identified during the security assessment of the AI coding assistant, with assigned owners, remediation timelines, and milestone tracking in the agency's GRC tool.

items.label: Review and accept the LLM provider's FedRAMP Customer Responsibility Matrix
items.description: Formally review the provider's shared responsibility documentation, sign off on all customer-inherited and shared controls, and assign internal owners for each agency-responsible control before deployment.

items.label: Validate that the AI agent's sandbox execution environment meets FIPS 140-2/140-3 encryption requirements
items.description: Confirm that all data at rest and in transit within the sandbox execution and test validation pipeline uses FIPS-validated cryptographic modules, documented with module certificate numbers.

sections.heading: Code Security, Vulnerability, and License Verification
sections.description: Establish automated gates in the AI-assisted development pipeline that enforce security scanning, dependency vetting, and license compliance before any LLM-generated or LLM-modified code reaches staging or production.

items.label: Integrate SAST scanning on all LLM-generated code before merge
items.description: Configure a Static Application Security Testing tool (e.g., Semgrep, Fortify, Checkmarx) as a mandatory CI/CD gate that scans every code suggestion accepted from the AI agent, blocking merges on high or critical findings.

items.label: Run software composition analysis (SCA) on all dependencies suggested by the AI agent
items.description: Use an SCA tool (e.g., OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk, Black Duck) to automatically scan any new package or library the AI agent recommends, blocking introduction of dependencies with known CVEs rated CVSS 7.0 or higher.

items.label: Enforce an approved dependency allowlist for government repositories
items.description: Maintain an agency-approved allowlist of vetted open-source libraries and versions; configure the AI agent's suggestion pipeline to flag or reject any dependency not on the allowlist, requiring security review before addition.

items.label: Scan all AI-suggested code for license compliance against agency-approved license types
items.description: Run automated license scanning (e.g., FOSSA, LicenseFinder) on every dependency and code snippet introduced by the AI agent, blocking GPL or other copyleft licenses incompatible with government software distribution policies.

items.label: Validate sandbox execution outputs against expected test results before accepting AI-generated code
items.description: Require that all AI-generated code pass the full existing test suite in the isolated sandbox environment with zero regressions before the agent's suggestion is surfaced to the developer as accepted output.

items.label: Implement a human-in-the-loop review requirement for AI-generated code touching authentication, authorization, or cryptography modules
items.description: Flag any AI-generated code that modifies authentication flows, access control logic, or cryptographic implementations for mandatory senior developer review before merge, with the review documented in the commit history.

items.label: Scan AI-generated infrastructure-as-code (IaC) templates for misconfigurations
items.description: Apply IaC security scanning tools (e.g., Checkov, tfsec) to any Terraform, CloudFormation, or Ansible content generated by the AI agent, blocking templates with open security groups, unencrypted storage, or missing logging configurations.

sections.heading: Explainability, Auditability & Section 508 Accessibility
sections.description: Ensure the AI coding assistant's outputs, decisions, and interactions are fully auditable for oversight bodies, explainable to developers and agency leadership, and accessible to users with disabilities as required by Section 508.

items.label: Log all AI agent interactions with immutable, tamper-evident audit trails
items.description: Capture every prompt, completion, code suggestion, and sandbox execution result in an append-only audit log stored in a SIEM or log management system with integrity verification, retained per agency records schedules.

items.label: Implement explainability metadata for every AI code suggestion surfaced to developers
items.description: Configure the agent to attach a brief rationale to each code suggestion—citing the relevant code pattern, security rule, or test failure it is addressing—so developers can evaluate and document why AI-generated code was accepted or rejected.

items.label: Establish a formal appeal and override process for AI agent code recommendations
items.description: Create a documented workflow allowing developers or security reviewers to formally override, escalate, or appeal AI agent suggestions, with each override logged with justification to satisfy the Executive Order on AI's explainability and appealability requirements.

items.label: Conduct Section 508 conformance testing on the AI coding assistant's user interface
items.description: Test the developer-facing UI of the AI coding assistant against WCAG 2.1 AA criteria and Section 508 standards, documenting results in a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) before agency-wide rollout.

items.label: Provide keyboard-navigable and screen-reader-compatible interfaces for all AI suggestion panels
items.description: Verify that all inline code suggestion displays, diff views, and agent chat interfaces are fully operable via keyboard alone and announce content changes to screen readers (NVDA, JAWS) without information loss.

items.label: Publish an AI use disclosure notice to agency staff and relevant oversight bodies
items.description: Issue a formal notice to all developers, IT leadership, and the agency's AI governance board documenting the AI coding assistant's capabilities, limitations, data handling practices, and the human oversight controls in place.

items.label: Schedule quarterly reviews of AI agent performance metrics and bias indicators
items.description: Establish a quarterly review cadence examining code acceptance rates, security finding rates on AI-generated vs. human-written code, and any patterns suggesting systematic errors or biased suggestions across programming languages or developer demographics.

items.label: Maintain an incident response runbook specific to AI coding assistant failures or misuse
items.description: Document a dedicated IR runbook covering scenarios including credential leakage via LLM output, introduction of malicious code through compromised AI suggestions, and unauthorized access to the agent's repository context, with escalation paths to CISA if warranted.

notes.note: Any commercial LLM API used by the coding assistant must appear on the FedRAMP Marketplace with an active authorization at the appropriate impact level (Moderate for CUI, High for sensitive national security systems). Using a non-FedRAMP-authorized LLM in a federal system constitutes a FISMA violation and can invalidate the system's ATO, exposing the agency to OIG findings and potential contract debarment.

The AI coding assistant must satisfy SA-11 (Developer Security Testing) by integrating automated SAST and SCA into the agent's suggestion pipeline, SI-3 (Malicious Code Protection) by scanning all sandbox-executed code, and SI-10 (Information Input Validation) by validating that LLM outputs do not introduce injection vulnerabilities or malformed inputs into government applications.

notes.regulation: Executive Order 14110 on Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy AI
notes.note: Federal agencies deploying AI coding assistants must complete an Algorithmic Impact Assessment documenting the system's decision logic, potential failure modes, and civil liberties implications. The EO also requires agencies to designate a Chief AI Officer accountable for the deployment and to report AI use cases to OMB, making pre-deployment documentation and governance structure non-negotiable before go-live.

notes.regulation: Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act (29 U.S.C. § 794d)
notes.note: All developer-facing interfaces of the AI coding assistant—including inline suggestion panels, diff views, chat interfaces, and configuration dashboards—must conform to the Revised 508 Standards (36 CFR Part 1194) and WCAG 2.1 AA. Agencies must obtain a completed VPAT from the vendor and conduct independent accessibility testing before procurement approval, as non-conformant tools expose the agency to Section 508 complaints and procurement challenges.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI coding assistant against FedRAMP, FISMA, and NIST 800-53 controls—turning this checklist into automated evidence collection, real-time alerts, and audit-ready reports for your Authorizing Official.
